content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 5, 2005

Bill Emmerson
Assemblyman, 63rd District
California State Assembly
State Capitol
P.O. Box 942849
Sacramento, CA 94249-0063

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-05-237

Dear Assemblyman Emmerson:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of San Bernardino City Councilmember Susan Lien Longville regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when issuing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Our advice is applicable only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and all of the material facts have been provided.

QUESTION

As a member of the San Bernardino City Council, may Councilmember Longville participate in a decision to implement a homebuyer assistance program for faculty of California State University San Bernardino?

CONCLUSION

Yes. Councilmember Longville may participate in a decision to implement a homebuyer assistance program for faculty of California State University San Bernardino.

_______________

¹ The Act is contained in Government Code sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in sections 18109 through 18997 of title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to title 2, division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-05-237
Page No. 2

FACTS

Susan Lien Longville is a member of the San Bernardino City Council. She is also
employed by California State University San Bernardino ("CSUSB") in an administrative
position in which she serves as the director of CSUSB's Water Resources Institute.

The San Bernardino City Counsel will soon be called upon to decide whether to
implement a homebuyers assistance program for the faculty of CSUSB. Under the proposed
program, new and current faculty of CSUSB, who do not presently own a home in San
Bernardino, would be able to obtain a loan of up to $70,000, at a 3% annual interest rate, from
the city's Economic Development Agency to assist them with the purchase of a home in the city.
The amount of the loan would be forgiven at the rate of 10% for every year that the faculty
member remains living in the home, and would only become due and payable, subject to the
forgiveness credit, if the faculty member, prior to the expiration of ten years, either sells the
home, refinances the home to receive cash, or ceases using the home as his or her primary
residence. The purpose of the program is to assist CSUSB in its efforts to recruit quality faculty
who might otherwise be dissuaded from coming to CSUSB due to the cost of area housing.

Councilmember Longville has stated, in a telephone conversation with FPPC staff, that as
an administrator at CSUSB she would not be eligible to participate in the homebuyers assistance
program if it is implemented, so neither she nor her spouse would be able to receive any benefit
from the program.

ANALYSIS

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act are intended to ensure that public officials
"perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial
interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (§ 81001, subd. (b).)
To that end, § 87100 specifically prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a financial
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public
official's economic interests. (§ 87103, Reg. 18700, subd. (a).) The Commission has adopted a
standard eight-step analysis for determining whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of
interest in a given governmental decision. (Reg. 18700, subd. (b)(1)-(8).) This letter applies the
eight-step analysis to the facts that you have presented to us, and renders advice based on the
results of that analysis.

Step One: Is Councilmember Longville a "public official?"

The term "public official" is defined by § 82048 of the Act, as follows:

"'Public official' means every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency, but does not

File No. A-05-237
Page No. 3

include judges and court commissioners in the judicial branch of
government."

As a member of the San Bernardino City Council, Councilmember Longville is certainly a public
official under the Act.

Step Two: Will Councilmember Longville be making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?

Regulation 18702.1, subdivision (a) defines "making a governmental decision," as
including voting on a matter, appointing a person, obligating or committing one's agency to a
course of action, entering into a contract or agreement on behalf of one's agency, and
determining not to act upon a matter by doing any of those things. Regulation 18702.2
subdivision (b)(2) defines what is meant by "participating in making a governmental decision" as
including advising or making recommendations to the decisionmaker by presenting any analysis
or opinion, orally or in writing, which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of an official
and the purpose of which is to influence a governmental decision. By considering
implementation of the homebuyers assistance program at a city council meeting, discussing the
proposed program with other members of the city council, and voting on whether to implement
the program, Councilmember Longville would be making and participating in a governmental
decision.

Step Three: What are Commissioner Laymon's economic interests?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from
economic interests. A public official has an economic interest in:

• A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $ 2,000 or more (§ 87103(a); reg. 18703.1(a)); or in
which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee or
holds any position of management (§ 87103(d); reg. 18703.1(b));

• Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of
$ 2,000 or more (§ 87103(b); reg. 18703.2);

• Any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(§ 87103(c); reg. 18703.3);

• Any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $360 or
more within 12 months prior to the decision (§ 87103(e); reg.
18703.4);

File No. A-05-237
Page No. 4

• His or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family this is the "personal financial effects" rule.
(§ 87103; reg. 18703.5.)

Based on the information contained in your letter requesting advice, it appears that
Councilmember Longville's concern about having a disqualifying conflict of interest arises from
her connection with CSUSB as a salaried administrative employee. However, not all employee
positions or sources of income are recognized as potentially disqualifying economic interests
under the Act.

While being an employee of a business entity constitutes having an economic interest in
the business entity under section 87103, subdivision (d) of the Act, being an employee of a
governmental entity does not constitute having an economic interest in the governmental entity.
Similarly, while receiving income of $500 or more from over a 12 month period from a single
source generally gives an official an economic interest in that source of income, such is not the
case if the source is a federal, state, or local government agency, as section 82030, subdivision
(b) of the Act specifically excludes such from the definition of "income" salary or wages
received from such governmental entities. As such, even though Councilmember Longville is a
salaried employee of CSUSB, she does not have an economic interest in CSUSB because it is an
agency of state government.

Therefore, all that remains to be considered is Councilmember Longville's economic
interest in her own personal finances. A public official always has an economic interest in his or
her personal finances (section 87103), which are defined to include the official's expenses,
income (except for income which is excluded from this definition such as governmental salary and benefits), assets and
liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. (Regulation 18703.5.)

Steps Four, Five, and Six: Is Councilmember Longville's economic interest in her personal
finances directly or indirectly involved in the decision? What is the standard for
determining if an effect of the decision on Councilmember Longville's economic interest in
her personal finances is material? Is it reasonably foreseeable that the decision would affect
Councilmember Longville's personal finances by $250 or more in a 12-month period?

The next steps in the analysis are, first, to determine whether the identified economic
interest will be involved directly or indirectly in the decision the councilmember would be
making and participating in making. The answer to this question determines the standard used to
determine the materiality standard applicable to the councilmember's economic interests, as
established by regulations 18705 et seq. Finally, after determining the level of economic effect
considered to be "material" for the economic interest, it must be determined whether it is
"reasonably foreseeable" that the effect of the decision on the economic interest will be
"material." (Reg. 18700, subd. (b)(6).)

Based on the facts provided, it is apparent that as Councilmember Longville would not be
eligible to participate in the proposed homebuyers assistance program, it is therefore not
reasonably foreseeable that a decision whether to implement the program would affect her

File No. A-05-237
Page No. 5

personal finances by $250 or more in a 12-month period. Since it is not reasonably foreseeable
that Councilmember Longville will be materially affected by the governmental decision, she may
participate in the decision regarding the homebuyers assistance program.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: [illegible]
Steven Benito Russo
Senior Commission Counsel
Legal Division
